title: Add Access Control visualization (RBAC/ABAC + broken access control attack)
description: OWASP #1 for 4+ years. Stanford L5, MIT, Berkeley all teach it. PortSwigger + Hacksplaining cover it. Essential consensus (8/8 sources). Build: (1) RBAC model — users → roles → permissions matrix, (2) ABAC model — attributes evaluated against policies, (3) Broken access control attack — user changes /api/users/123 to /api/users/456, (4) Defense with proper authorization checks. Sources: OWASP A01:2025, Stanford CS155 L5, PortSwigger Access Control labs.
acceptanceCriteria: New topic 'Access Control' appears in sidebar with Intermediate difficulty
RBAC visualization shows users mapped to roles mapped to permissions
Broken access control attack with 5+ step flow
Defense demo showing proper authorization check

title: Add Session Management visualization (cookie lifecycle, session fixation, JWT vs cookies)
description: Essential consensus (7/8). Stanford L10, MIT, Berkeley teach it. Top student confusion: 'JWT vs session cookies.' Build: (1) Cookie-based session lifecycle with HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite flags, (2) Session fixation attack — attacker sets session ID before login, (3) Session hijacking via XSS stealing cookie, (4) JWT vs Session Cookie side-by-side comparison with trade-offs. Sources: OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet, Stanford CS155 L10.
acceptanceCriteria: New topic 'Session Management' appears in sidebar
Cookie lifecycle shows Set-Cookie → send → expire flow
Session fixation attack with step-by-step
JWT vs Cookie comparison with trade-off table: scalability, revocation, XSS risk, size

title: Fix AES MixColumns to match NIST FIPS-197 (P0 accuracy fix)
description: AES MixColumns at aes-engine.ts:192-208 is mathematically incorrect. Output[0][c] only depends on 2 of 4 bytes (a0,a1), not all 4 as documented. Real AES multiplies by [[2,3,1,1],[1,2,3,1],[1,1,2,3],[3,1,1,2]] in GF(2^8). Students learning wrong math. Source: FIPS-197 §5.1.3. NOTE: This duplicates SEC-019 from mega audit — keeping for curriculum tracking.
acceptanceCriteria: MixColumns uses proper GF(2^8) xtime() multiplication
NIST Appendix B test vector produces correct output
Each output byte depends on ALL 4 input bytes in column

title: Fix certificate chain 'HMAC' → 'digital signature' (P0 accuracy fix)
description: cert-chain.ts:184 says 'HMAC(serverCert, intermediateCA.publicKey)'. HMAC is symmetric; cert verification uses asymmetric digital signatures (RSA/ECDSA). Source: RFC 5280 §6.1. NOTE: Duplicates SEC-020 — keeping for curriculum tracking.
acceptanceCriteria: No 'HMAC' in cert-chain step descriptions
Correctly describes RSA/ECDSA signature verification

title: Fix password hashing description claiming 'Blowfish-based' when using FNV-1a
description: password-hashing.ts:140 says 'the Blowfish-based key schedule processes the password+salt.' But the actual simpleHash function at line 48-64 uses FNV-1a, not Blowfish. Either (a) implement a simplified Blowfish-like key schedule, or (b) change description to 'a hash function processes the password+salt' without claiming Blowfish. Source: bcrypt paper (Provos & Mazières 1999).
acceptanceCriteria: Description accurately reflects the hash function being used
No false claim about Blowfish if implementation doesn't use it
Educational value of 'intentionally slow' concept preserved

title: Add Supply Chain Security visualization (dependency graph, typosquatting, SBOM)
description: Important consensus (5 sources). OWASP #3 (NEW 2025). Stanford CS155 L13 dedicated guest lecture. CISA 2025 SBOM requirements. EU Cyber Resilience Act. Build: (1) Dependency tree showing transitive dependencies, (2) Three attack vectors: typosquatting ('lodash' vs 'lodas'), dependency confusion (private vs public registry), build pipeline compromise, (3) SBOM structure and why it's now legally mandated. Sources: OWASP A03:2025, CISA SBOM 2025, Stanford CS155 L13.
acceptanceCriteria: New topic 'Supply Chain Security' in sidebar
Dependency tree visualization with attack propagation
3 attack scenarios with step-by-step flows
SBOM concept with 9 minimum data fields (CISA 2025)

title: Add API Security visualization (BOLA, BFLA, mass assignment)
description: Important consensus (5 sources). OWASP API Top 10 #1 = BOLA. API attacks surged 300%+ YoY. Build: (1) BOLA — user changes /api/orders/123 to /api/orders/456, server doesn't check ownership, (2) BFLA — regular user calls admin endpoint, (3) Mass assignment — attacker adds 'role: admin' to POST body. Sources: OWASP API Security Top 10 2023, PortSwigger API Testing.
acceptanceCriteria: New topic 'API Security' in sidebar
BOLA attack with request modification visualization
BFLA attack with unauthorized function call
Mass assignment with extra payload fields
Defense patterns for each attack

title: Add Passkeys/WebAuthn visualization (registration + authentication ceremony)
description: Emerging consensus (5 sources). 87% enterprise deployment 2026. NIST SP 800-63-4 requires phishing-resistant auth. MIT includes in labs. Build: (1) Registration ceremony — browser generates key pair, sends public key, (2) Authentication ceremony — server challenge, authenticator signs, server verifies, (3) Comparison with password-based login. Sources: FIDO Alliance 2025 report, NIST SP 800-63-4.
acceptanceCriteria: New topic 'Passkeys / WebAuthn' in sidebar
Registration ceremony with key pair generation flow
Authentication ceremony with challenge-response
Password vs Passkey comparison highlighting security improvements

title: Add Zero Trust Architecture visualization (PDP/PEP flow)
description: Emerging consensus (4 sources). Gartner: 60% orgs replacing VPN by 2026. DoD zero trust implementation primer Jan 2026. NIST SP 800-207. Build: (1) Traditional perimeter model (castle-and-moat), (2) Zero Trust model with PDP/PEP, (3) Three core principles animated: verify explicitly, least privilege, assume breach, (4) Comparison showing why perimeter fails with remote work/cloud. Sources: NIST SP 800-207, CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model, Microsoft Zero Trust.
acceptanceCriteria: New topic 'Zero Trust' in sidebar
Traditional vs Zero Trust side-by-side comparison
PDP/PEP request evaluation flow animated
Three principles visually demonstrated

title: Add Clickjacking attack to Web Attacks topic
description: Important (4 sources). Stanford CS155 L9. PortSwigger + Hacksplaining both cover it. Build: transparent iframe overlay attack with step-by-step flow. Defense: X-Frame-Options, frame-ancestors CSP. Sources: PortSwigger Clickjacking labs, OWASP Clickjacking.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Clickjacking' tab in Web Attacks
Attack flow shows iframe overlay concept

title: Add Command Injection attack to Web Attacks topic
description: Important (4 sources). Stanford CS155 L9. PortSwigger + Hacksplaining cover it. Build: attacker injects OS command via user input (e.g., '; rm -rf /'). Defense: parameterized system calls, input whitelist validation. Sources: PortSwigger Command Injection, OWASP.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Command Injection' tab in Web Attacks
Attack flow shows OS command injection via user input
Defense with parameterized calls and input validation

title: Add Open Redirect attack to Web Attacks topic
description: Important (4 sources). Stanford + PortSwigger + Hacksplaining. Attacker crafts URL like https://bank.com/redirect?url=https://evil.com. Victim trusts bank.com domain. Defense: URL whitelist. Sources: PortSwigger Open Redirect, OWASP.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Open Redirect' tab in Web Attacks
Attack shows URL crafting and redirect chain
Defense shows URL whitelist validation

title: Add Security Headers interactive dashboard (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
description: Important (4 sources). Security Misconfiguration is OWASP #2 in 2025. CSP code exists in csp.ts but has no visualization. Build interactive builder: toggle each directive, see what it prevents. Live preview. Copy-to-clipboard. Sources: OWASP A02:2025, OWASP Secure Headers Project.
acceptanceCriteria: New topic 'Security Headers' in sidebar
Interactive CSP builder with directive toggles

title: Add AI/LLM Security visualization (prompt injection, OWASP LLM Top 10)
description: Emerging (4 sources). OWASP LLM Top 10 #1 = Prompt Injection. Stanford CS155 L18 dedicated lecture (new 2026). PortSwigger added 'Web LLM attacks'. Build: (1) Direct prompt injection — user manipulates system prompt, (2) Indirect prompt injection — malicious content in external data processed by LLM, (3) Defense: input validation, output filtering, privilege restrictions. First-mover opportunity. Sources: OWASP LLM01:2025, Stanford CS155 L18.
acceptanceCriteria: New topic 'AI/LLM Security' in sidebar

title: Add origin/history stories for cryptographic topics
description: Zero history across all topics. Add brief origin stories: DH → 'Invented 1976 by Diffie & Hellman, revolutionized crypto by solving key distribution without meeting in person', AES → 'Won NIST competition in 2001 as Rijndael, beating Twofish and Serpent', OAuth → 'Created because Twitter needed third-party app authorization without sharing passwords (2007)', bcrypt → 'Designed 1999 by Provos & Mazières specifically to be SLOW — opposite of every other hash'. Source: original papers + Wikipedia.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 6 topics have a 'History' or 'Origin' section

Build: (1) Why quantum breaks RSA/DH (Shor's algorithm concept), (2) Lattice-based crypto intuition, (3) Key size comparison: RSA-2048 vs ML-KEM-768. Source: NIST PQC standards, Cloudflare PQC blog.

title: Add difficulty tags and recommended learning order to all topics
description: Topics are listed without difficulty indicators or pedagogical ordering. Based on curriculum analysis, recommended order: Password Hashing (Beginner) → Encryption Comparison (Beginner) → AES (Advanced) → DH (Intermediate) → HTTPS Flow (Intermediate) → Cert Chain (Intermediate) → JWT (Beginner) → OAuth (Intermediate) → CORS (Intermediate) → Web Attacks (Intermediate) → Rate Limiting (Intermediate). Source: Berkeley CS161 progression.
acceptanceCriteria: Each topic has visible difficulty badge (Beginner/Intermediate/Advanced)

title: Add prerequisite links between topics
description: No cross-references between topics. Add 'Prerequisite' and 'Next Steps' links: DH requires understanding of modular arithmetic → link to Encryption Comparison first. HTTPS Flow builds on DH + AES + Cert Chain. OAuth uses JWT. Web Attacks defense uses CSP (Security Headers). Source: dependency analysis from curriculum paths.

title: Add rate limiting 'when to use which' comparison guide
description: Rate limiting shows 3 algorithms but never explains when to use which. Add decision guide: Token Bucket = bursty traffic allowed (API rate limiting), Sliding Window = strict per-window limits (login attempts), Leaky Bucket = smoothed output rate (message queues). Source: Google Cloud Rate Limiting docs, Kong rate limiting docs.
acceptanceCriteria: Decision guide visible in rate limiting properties panel or canvas
Each algorithm has a 'Best for:' and 'Not ideal for:' section
Specific real-world use cases named for each

title: Add CORS vs CSP comparison (top student confusion point)
description: CORS and CSP are the #1 most confused security concepts per Reddit and Medium research. Build side-by-side: CORS protects server resources from unauthorized cross-origin access (server-side), CSP protects the page from loading unauthorized resources (client-side). Same scenario from both perspectives. Source: Medium 'Understanding CORS, CSRF & XSS' article, reddit threads.
acceptanceCriteria: Comparison view accessible from CORS topic
Side-by-side layout: CORS (left) vs CSP (right)
Same scenario shown from both perspectives
Key differences highlighted with visual callouts

title: Implement FAANG Interview Prep learning path
description: Based on 2026 interview research, build a 4-week structured learning path: Week 1 (Auth): OAuth, JWT, Sessions, Passkeys. Week 2 (Web): XSS, CSRF, SQLi, CORS, CSP, Clickjacking. Week 3 (Crypto): HTTPS, Certs, Encryption, Hashing. Week 4 (Architecture): Access Control, Threat Modeling, API Security, Zero Trust. Track completion per week. Source: InterviewKickstart, Exponent, NeetCode security topics.
